intro.why: Historical simulation (LO 1.a) is simple but has two soft spots: it only uses the one sample you happen to have, and it treats every observation in the window as equally important. This chapter is about squeezing more out of the same data (bootstrapping, density smoothing) and making the sample better reflect "now" (weighting schemes) — all while staying true to the non-parametric spirit of "let the data decide."
intro.content:
The four learning outcomes split into two pairs:

  • Bootstrap Historical Simulation and Non-Parametric Density Estimation both ask "how do I get more out of the same sample?" — one by resampling for precision, the other by smoothing for finer confidence levels.
  • Weighted Historical Simulation asks a different question: "is every observation in my window really equally important?" — and offers four answers (age, volatility, correlation, filtered).
  • Advantages and Disadvantages ties it together: every strength and weakness of these data-driven methods traces back to one root cause — everything depends on the historical sample.

Throughline: this chapter is a toolbox of fixes for the two soft spots of plain historical simulation (LO 1.a) — precision/resolution on one side, representativeness on the other — without ever assuming a distribution.

los.lo: LO 2.a — Apply the bootstrap historical simulation approach to estimate coherent risk measures
los.title: Bootstrap Historical Simulation
los.why: A single historical-simulation VaR is at the mercy of exactly which observations happen to sit near your tail cutoff in this one sample. Bootstrapping asks "what if I had gotten a slightly different sample from the same world?" — many times — and averages away that sample-specific noise to get a more precise estimate.
los.keyIdea: Resample your historical data with replacement many times, compute VaR/ES on each resample, and average the results — the original sample's own VaR plays no role in the final answer.
los.content:
The procedure

  1. Start with the original historical data set of n observations.
  2. Draw a random sample from this data set with replacement — after an observation is drawn it goes back into the pool, so it can be drawn again.
  3. Compute the risk measure (VaR, or ES via the tail-slicing procedure from LO 1.c) on this resample.
  4. Repeat steps 2–3 many times, producing many sample risk-measure estimates.
  5. The final estimate is the average across all the resampled risk measures.

Critically, the VaR/ES computed from the original, unmodified data set plays no role in the final answer — only the resampled estimates are averaged.

Why averaging over resamples helps

A single historical-simulation VaR depends heavily on exactly which observations happen to fall near the tail boundary in the one sample you have. Bootstrapping effectively asks "if I had gotten a slightly different sample from the same underlying process, what would my VaR estimate have looked like?" — many times over — and averages out that sample-to-sample noise. Empirically, this consistently produces more precise estimates of coherent risk measures (including VaR and ES as special cases) than relying on the single VaR/ES from the raw, un-resampled data.

Relationship to the rest of the lesson

Bootstrapping is a technique for using a historical data set more effectively — it doesn't, by itself, address which historical observations should be included or how they should be weighted. That's the role of weighted historical simulation (LO 2.c). The two ideas combine: filtered historical simulation explicitly uses bootstrapping as one of its steps.
takeaways: Resampling with replacement from your own data, many times, then averaging → a more precise VaR/ES.
The original sample's own VaR/ES is discarded — only the resampled estimates matter.
Bootstrapping improves precision; it does not address which observations should count more (→ weighted HS).
Filtered historical simulation (LO 2.c) explicitly builds on this bootstrapping step.

los.lo: LO 2.b — Describe historical simulation using non-parametric density estimation
los.title: Non-Parametric Density Estimation
los.why: Plain historical simulation can only give you VaR at confidence levels that land exactly on a data point — with 100 observations, there is no clean way to get VaR at 95.5%. Smoothing the histogram into a continuous curve fixes this without adding any new distributional assumptions.
los.keyIdea: Connect the midpoints of histogram bar-tops into a continuous "surrogate density function" — area-conserving, so it stays a valid distribution but lets you read off VaR at any confidence level.
los.content:
The discreteness problem

With n historical observations, plain historical simulation can only distinguish n different confidence levels. With 100 observations you can estimate VaR at the 95% or 96% level (the 5th or 4th worst observation), but there's no well-defined way to estimate VaR at 95.5% — no observation sits exactly there. More observations help (more achievable confidence levels), but the underlying problem — a finite, discrete sample implying a step-function distribution — never goes away on its own.

The fix: a surrogate density function

The non-parametric fix doesn't add any new assumption about the shape of the distribution — it just connects the dots of the existing histogram into a continuous curve.

The simplest version: take the histogram of historical observations, and instead of treating each bar as a flat block, connect the midpoint of the top of each bar to the midpoint of the top of the next bar with a straight line segment. The result is a surrogate density function — a piecewise-linear curve approximating the true (unknown) density.

A key property: connecting the midpoints shifts area between adjacent bars but doesn't create or destroy any — a taller bar "donates" some area to a shorter neighbor, and vice versa, but the total area under the curve is conserved. So the surrogate function remains a valid probability distribution (it still integrates to 1), just smoother than the original step-histogram.

Because the surrogate density is continuous rather than a step function, you can now identify a cutoff — and therefore a VaR estimate — at any confidence level, including ones strictly between two adjacent historical observations.

Beyond linear connections

Connecting midpoints with straight lines is the simplest possible smoothing rule, but not the only one. More sophisticated versions connect successive bars with curves, tracking the shape of the underlying data more faithfully at the cost of complexity. The linear version is most often discussed because it illustrates the core idea — continuity from a discrete sample — without extra machinery.
takeaways: n observations → only n representable confidence levels in plain historical simulation.
The "surrogate density function" connects histogram bar-top midpoints into a continuous curve.
Area is conserved (still integrates to 1) — this is smoothing, not a new distributional assumption.
Lets you read VaR off at any confidence level, not just ones the raw data happens to hit.

los.lo: LO 2.c — Compare and contrast the age-weighted, the volatility-weighted, the correlation-weighted, and the filtered historical simulation approaches
los.title: Weighted Historical Simulation Approaches
los.why: Plain historical simulation treats every day in the window as equally important and every day outside it as irrelevant — a hard, arbitrary cutoff that creates "ghost effects" and ignores that markets have memory and shifting volatility regimes. These four approaches each relax the equal-weighting assumption in a different way.
los.keyIdea: Four different ways to stop treating every historical observation as equally important: reweight by recency, by volatility, by the full covariance matrix, or all of the above together.
los.content:
The problem with equal weighting

Plain historical simulation gives every observation inside the estimation window an equal weight of 1/n, and every observation outside it a weight of zero. This has three specific weaknesses:

  1. Arbitrary cutoff — an observation from n days ago counts fully; from n+1 days ago, not at all. There's no principled reason importance should drop off a cliff at exactly that point.
  2. "Ghost effects" — a single extreme historical event affects the VaR estimate at full weight for the entire window, then disappears abruptly once it ages out, producing a sudden jump in VaR with no corresponding change in market conditions.
  3. i.i.d. assumption — equal weighting implicitly assumes observations are independent and identically distributed, but real return data often shows seasonality (volatility clustering, time-varying correlations).

The four approaches below each target one or more of these weaknesses.

Age-weighted

Changes: how much weight an observation gets, based on how old it is. Rather than a hard 1/n-vs-0 cutoff, age-weighting assigns exponentially decaying weights: the most recent observation gets the largest weight, and each successively older observation's weight is the previous one multiplied by a decay parameter λ (0 ≤ λ ≤ 1). Weights sum to 1 across the sample.

  • λ close to 1 → slow decay → close to equal weighting (λ = 1 is equal weighting).
  • λ close to 0 → fast decay → recent observations dominate almost entirely.

Fixes: ghost effects (an old extreme event fades gradually instead of vanishing all at once) and softens the arbitrary cutoff (no hard edge — weight just gets very small for very old data). Sometimes called the "hybrid approach."

Volatility-weighted

Changes: the magnitude of each historical return, based on the volatility prevailing when it occurred versus the volatility prevailing now. Rather than reweighting probabilities, this rescales the returns themselves — each historical return is multiplied by (current volatility forecast) / (volatility forecast on the day that return occurred), both typically from a GARCH or EWMA model. After rescaling, VaR/ES is computed exactly as before, using the volatility-adjusted returns.

Intuition: if volatility has recently risen, old returns (from a calmer period) are too small relative to what's likely now — scaling up corrects this. If volatility has recently fallen, old returns are too large — scaling down corrects this.

Fixes: the i.i.d./seasonality problem directly. A side-benefit: because old high-volatility returns get scaled up when current volatility is elevated, this approach can produce VaR estimates larger than any individual loss actually observed in the raw historical data — something plain historical simulation can never do.

Correlation-weighted

Changes: the same rescaling idea, but applied to the full variance-covariance matrix rather than just individual variances. The matrix Σ has diagonal elements (each asset's own variance) and off-diagonal elements (covariances between asset pairs). Volatility-weighting updates only the diagonal; correlation-weighting updates the entire matrix — both variances and covariances — and uses the updated matrix to produce correlation-adjusted returns.

Fixes: everything volatility-weighting fixes, plus the possibility that the relationships between assets (not just their individual volatilities) have shifted — a richer, more complex tool for multi-asset portfolios.

Filtered historical simulation

Changes: combines several of the above ideas — the most comprehensive (and most complex) of the four.

  1. Fit a conditional volatility model (e.g. GARCH, possibly an asymmetric variant capturing a "surprise" effect) to the historical return series, producing a volatility forecast for each day.
  2. Standardize each historical return by dividing by its corresponding volatility forecast — stripping out time-varying volatility, leaving a series closer to i.i.d.
  3. Bootstrap (resample with replacement, per LO 2.a) from this standardized series to simulate returns, then rescale using the current volatility forecast.
  4. Read VaR (or ES) off the resulting simulated distribution.

Fixes: by combining a volatility model with bootstrapped historical simulation, filtered HS captures conditional volatility, volatility clustering, and asymmetric "surprise" effects — addressing the i.i.d. problem as thoroughly as any of these four, while remaining grounded in the historical-simulation framework. It extends to longer holding periods and multi-asset portfolios, and stays computationally reasonable even for large portfolios. The tradeoff: an extra step (fitting a volatility model) on top of the historical simulation machinery.

Summary comparison

ApproachReweights...Mainly addresses
Age-weightedprobabilities (by recency)arbitrary cutoff, ghost effects
Volatility-weightedreturn magnitudes (by volatility ratio)i.i.d. / changing volatility
Correlation-weightedreturn magnitudes (by full covariance matrix)i.i.d. / changing volatility and correlation
Filteredboth — standardizes, bootstraps, rescalesall of the above, most comprehensively
los.formula:
Age weight: w(i) = λ^(i−1)(1 − λ) / (1 − λ^n)
Volatility-adjusted return: r*_(t,i) = (σ_(T,i) / σ_(t,i)) × r_(t,i)
takeaways: Age-weighted: reweights probability by recency via exponential decay λ; λ = 1 reduces to equal weighting.
Volatility-weighted: rescales the returns themselves by (current vol / vol on that day) — can exceed any historical loss.
Correlation-weighted: same idea as volatility-weighting but for the whole covariance matrix (multi-asset).
Filtered HS: standardize by a GARCH vol forecast, bootstrap, rescale by current vol — most comprehensive, most complex.

los.lo: LO 2.d — Identify advantages and disadvantages of non-parametric estimation methods
los.title: Non-Parametric Methods — Pros and Cons
los.why: Every non-parametric method in this lesson (and plain historical simulation) shares one root trait: the result is only as good as the historical sample behind it. That single fact explains almost all of their strengths and all of their weaknesses — the lens for comparing them to parametric methods.
los.keyIdea: Strengths and weaknesses both trace back to one root cause: everything depends on the historical sample.
los.content:
Advantages

  • Conceptually simple and computationally light — sorting, slicing, averaging, resampling can all be done on a spreadsheet, no specialized software needed.
  • No fight with skewness or fat tails — because no distribution is assumed, these methods automatically reflect whatever shape the actual data has. Contrast with parametric VaR (LO 1.b), which can be biased if the assumed distribution doesn't match reality (LO 1.f).
  • No variance-covariance matrix headaches — parametric multi-asset VaR requires estimating and inverting a covariance matrix, which gets unwieldy as assets grow. Non-parametric methods sidestep this (with the partial exception of correlation-weighting, which reintroduces a covariance matrix — but as an updatable input, not a structural requirement).
  • Works with readily available data — historical price/return series are easy to obtain, with no special adjustments needed.
  • Composable — these techniques aren't mutually exclusive: you can combine age-weighting with volatility-weighting, or layer bootstrapping on top of any weighting scheme, as filtered historical simulation does.

Disadvantages

All of the following trace back to the same root issue: the result is only as good as the historical sample it's built from.

  • Critical dependence on historical data — if the sample doesn't represent the future well, neither will the risk estimate; there's no model to fall back on.
  • Volatile historical periods → VaR/ES too high for current, calmer conditions.
  • Quiet historical periods → VaR/ES too low for current, more volatile conditions. (Volatility-weighting and filtered HS specifically target this and the previous point.)
  • Slow to detect structural breaks or regime changes — by construction these methods describe the past, and a genuine regime change only shows up gradually as new data accumulates (or not at all, for age-weighting with λ close to 1).
  • Cannot imagine losses that have not happened yet — if a plausible but extreme event never occurred during the sample period, plain historical simulation has no mechanism to produce a loss estimate beyond the worst observed loss. (Volatility-weighting partially escapes this, since rescaling can push a historical return beyond its original magnitude.)
  • Data-hungry — a long, clean history is needed for the estimates to be meaningful, a problem for new instruments, new markets, or short trading histories.
takeaways: Strength: no distributional assumption → automatically reflects skew and fat tails (contrast with LO 1.b + LO 1.f).
Strength: simple, composable, and mostly free of covariance-matrix headaches.
Weakness: volatile history → VaR too high for calm markets now; quiet history → VaR too low for volatile markets now.
Weakness: cannot imagine a loss that has never happened, and slow to catch regime changes.
Many of LO 2.c's weighting schemes exist specifically to patch these weaknesses.
